File operations, bash, search, and web tools.

The `coding` server provides tools for reading and writing files, running shell commands, searching codebases, and fetching web content. See [Tools](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/) for configuration.

## File operations

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `read_file` | Read a file with optional line range (max 2000 lines per call) |
| `write_file` | Write content to a file (creates or overwrites) |
| `edit_file` | Find-and-replace within a file |
| `list_files` | List directory contents with metadata |

**`read_file(path, line_offset?, limit?)`**
- `path` — File path (relative to root)
- `line_offset` — Start line (0-indexed, optional)
- `limit` — Max lines to return (default: 2000)

**`write_file(path, content)`**
- `path` — File path
- `content` — Full file content

**`edit_file(path, find, replace, replace_all?)`**
- `path` — File path
- `find` — Text to search for (must be unique unless `replace_all` is true)
- `replace` — Replacement text
- `replace_all` — Replace all occurrences (default: false)

:::caution
`write_file` and `edit_file` require the file to be read first with `read_file`. This prevents agents from blindly overwriting files they haven't seen.
:::

## Search

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `grep` | Regex search across files with context and filtering |
| `find` | Find files by glob pattern |

**`grep(pattern, glob?, file_type?, context?, case_insensitive?, multiline?)`**
- `pattern` — Regex pattern (ripgrep syntax)
- `glob` — Filter files by glob (e.g., `"*.rs"`)
- `file_type` — Filter by type (e.g., `"rust"`, `"js"`)
- `context` — Lines of context around matches
- `case_insensitive` — Ignore case (default: false)
- `multiline` — Match across lines (default: false)

Output modes: `Content` (matching lines), `FilesWithMatches` (file paths only), `Count` (match counts).

**`find(pattern)`**
- `pattern` — Glob pattern (e.g., `"**/*.rs"`, `"src/**/test_*"`)

## Bash

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `bash` | Execute a shell command |
| `read_background_bash` | Read output from a background process |

**`bash(command, timeout?, background?)`**
- `command` — Shell command to execute
- `timeout` — Max duration in milliseconds (default: 120000, max: 600000)
- `background` — Run in background and return a shell ID (default: false)

**`read_background_bash(shell_id, regex?)`**
- `shell_id` — ID from a background bash call
- `regex` — Filter output lines by pattern

## Web

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `web_fetch` | Fetch a URL and convert HTML to markdown |
| `web_search` | Search the web via Brave Search API |

**`web_fetch(url)`**
- `url` — URL to fetch

**`web_search(query, allow_domains?, block_domains?)`**
- `query` — Search query
- `allow_domains` — Only return results from these domains
- `block_domains` — Exclude results from these domains

`web_search` requires the `BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY` environment variable.

## LSP (Language Server Protocol)

The `coding` server includes LSP tools by default. These same tools are also available as a standalone [LSP server](/aether/built-in-servers/lsp/) for agents that only need code intelligence.

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `lsp_symbol` | Go-to-definition, find references, hover, call hierarchy |
| `lsp_document` | Get all symbols in a document |
| `lsp_check_errors` | Get compiler diagnostics |
| `lsp_rename` | Rename a symbol across the codebase |

**`lsp_symbol(file, line, char, action)`**
- `action` — One of: `definition`, `references`, `hover`, `incoming_calls`, `outgoing_calls`
- `file` — File path
- `line` — Line number (0-indexed)
- `char` — Character offset (0-indexed)

**`lsp_document(file)`**
- `file` — File path

**`lsp_check_errors(file?)`**
- `file` — Optional file to check (omit for all workspace errors)

**`lsp_rename(file, line, char, new_name)`**
- `file` — File path
- `line` — Line number (0-indexed)
- `char` — Character offset (0-indexed)
- `new_name` — New name for the symbol
Language-aware code intelligence tools powered by LSP.

The `lsp` server provides language-aware code intelligence — symbol lookup, diagnostics, document structure, workspace search, and rename — powered by Language Server Protocol servers.

:::note
The `coding` server also includes these LSP tools when enabled. Use the standalone `lsp` server when you want code intelligence without file I/O, bash, or search tools.
:::

## Tools

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `lsp_symbol` | Go to definition, find references, hover info, implementations, call hierarchy |
| `lsp_document` | List all symbols in a file (functions, structs, modules) |
| `lsp_check_errors` | Get compiler errors and warnings without running a build |
| `lsp_workspace_search` | Search for symbols across the entire workspace by name |
| `lsp_rename` | Rename a symbol across the entire workspace |

### lsp_symbol

Navigate code by looking up definitions, references, implementations, hover docs, and call hierarchy.

**`lsp_symbol(operation, file_path, symbol, line?, include_declaration?, limit?, context_lines?)`**

- `operation` — One of: `definition`, `implementation`, `references`, `hover`, `incoming_calls`, `outgoing_calls`
- `file_path` — Absolute path to the file
- `symbol` — Exact symbol name
- `line` — 1-indexed line number (optional; skips auto-resolution if provided)
- `include_declaration` — Include the declaration site in references results (default: true)
- `limit` — Cap the number of results
- `context_lines` — Include N lines of source around each result

### lsp_document

Get the full symbol map of a file — functions, structs, traits, modules, etc. — with hierarchical structure.

**`lsp_document(file_path)`**

- `file_path` — Absolute path to the file

Use this before reading large files to identify which sections are relevant.

### lsp_check_errors

Get instant compiler errors and warnings without running a build. Prefer this over `cargo check`, `tsc`, `go build`, etc.

**`lsp_check_errors(file_path?)`**

- `file_path` — Absolute path to a file for single-file diagnostics. Omit for workspace-wide diagnostics.

Returns error/warning counts and formatted diagnostics with file paths and line numbers.

### lsp_workspace_search

Search for symbols across the entire workspace when you don't know which file they live in. Supports fuzzy and substring matching.

**`lsp_workspace_search(query, limit?, context_lines?)`**

- `query` — Symbol name to search for
- `limit` — Cap the number of results
- `context_lines` — Include N lines of source around each result

### lsp_rename

Rename a symbol across the entire workspace. All references are updated in a single operation.

**`lsp_rename(file_path, symbol, new_name, line?)`**

- `file_path` — Absolute path to the file containing the symbol
- `symbol` — Current symbol name
- `new_name` — New symbol name
- `line` — 1-indexed line number (optional; skips auto-resolution if provided)

Returns the list of files affected and total edits applied.
Create slash commands and reusable skill prompts.

import { Tabs, TabItem, FileTree } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The `skills` server lets you define slash commands and skill prompts that agents can load on demand. Commands are quick actions triggered by `/name`; skills are deeper knowledge bases agents can reference. See [Tools](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/) for configuration.

## Directory structure

<FileTree>
- .aether/ (or ~/.aether/ for global)
  - commands/
    - commit.md
    - review.md
    - plan.md
  - skills/
    - rust/
      - SKILL.md
      - traits.md
      - error-handling.md
    - testing/
      - SKILL.md
</FileTree>

## Tools

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `get_skills` | Load a skill or auxiliary file |
| `save_note` | Append content to a topic-based note |
| `search_notes` | Search notes by topic or tags |

<Tabs>
  <TabItem label="Writing a command">
    Commands are markdown files in the `commands/` directory. The filename (minus `.md`) becomes the command name.

    ```markdown title="commands/commit.md"
    ---
    description: Generate a commit message for staged changes
    argument-hint: [optional context]
    allowed-tools:
      - bash
      - grep
    ---

    Generate a concise commit message for the currently staged git changes.

    The user provided: $ARGUMENTS

    Rules:
    - Use conventional commit format
    - Keep the subject line under 72 characters
    ```

    ### Frontmatter fields

    | Field | Description |
    |-------|-------------|
    | `description` | Shown in the command picker UI |
    | `argument-hint` | Placeholder text for the argument input |
    | `allowed-tools` | Tools the agent can use when running this command |

    ### Template variables

    | Variable | Description |
    |----------|-------------|
    | `$ARGUMENTS` | The full argument string passed by the user |
    | `$1`, `$2`, ... | Positional arguments (space-separated) |
  </TabItem>
  <TabItem label="Writing a skill">
    Skills are directories containing a `SKILL.md` file and optional auxiliary files.

    ```markdown title="skills/rust/SKILL.md"
    ---
    name: rust
    description: Rust coding conventions and best practices
    tags: [rust, coding, testing]
    ---

    # Rust Conventions

    ## Error Handling
    - Use specific enum types, not `Box<dyn Error>`
    - Never add `anyhow` as a dependency

    ## Testing
    - Use `Fake` prefix, never `Mock`
    - Prefer in-memory fakes over side-effect mocks

    See [error-handling](./error-handling.md) for detailed patterns.
    ```

    ### SKILL.md frontmatter

    | Field | Description |
    |-------|-------------|
    | `name` | Skill identifier |
    | `description` | What this skill covers (used for discovery) |
    | `tags` | Search tags for finding relevant skills |

    ### Auxiliary files

    Additional markdown files in the skill directory can be loaded on demand:

    ```
    get_skills(name: "rust", path: "error-handling.md")
    ```
  </TabItem>
</Tabs>

## Global vs project skills

- **Project skills** — Place in `.aether/commands/` and `.aether/skills/` within your project
- **Global skills** — Place in `~/.aether/commands/` and `~/.aether/skills/` for availability across all projects
Spawn child agents for parallel execution.

The `subagents` server lets agents spawn other agents as children, enabling parallel work and delegation. See [Tools](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/) for configuration.

## Tools

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `spawn_subagent` | Spawn one or more child agents in parallel |

### spawn_subagent

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `tasks` | `array` | List of tasks to spawn |
| `tasks[].agent_name` | `string` | Name of the agent to spawn (must have `agentInvocable: true`) |
| `tasks[].prompt` | `string` | The prompt/instructions for the child agent |

## Configuration

Agents available for spawning must have `agentInvocable: true` in [settings.json](/aether/configuration/agent-settings/):

```json title=".aether/settings.json"
{
  "agents": [
    {
      "name": "coder",
      "description": "General coding agent",
      "model": "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5",
      "userInvocable": true,
      "agentInvocable": true
    },
    {
      "name": "researcher",
      "description": "Read-only research agent",
      "model": "anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5",
      "agentInvocable": true,
      "tools": {
        "allow": ["coding__read_file", "coding__grep", "coding__find", "coding__web_*"]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

## Example

A parent agent might spawn multiple researchers in parallel:

```json
{
  "tasks": [
    {
      "agent_name": "researcher",
      "prompt": "Find all database queries in the codebase and list them"
    },
    {
      "agent_name": "researcher",
      "prompt": "Read the README and summarize the project architecture"
    }
  ]
}
```

Each child agent runs independently with its own model, prompts, and tool access. Results are returned to the parent when all children complete.

## Output structure

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `summary` | High-level summary of what the agent did |
| `artifacts` | Any files created or modified |
| `decisions` | Key decisions made during execution |
| `next_steps` | Suggested follow-up actions |
Allow agents to ask structured questions via forms.
Hierarchical task management for complex work.

The `tasks` server gives agents a structured way to break down complex work into trackable tasks with dependencies and status tracking. Tasks are stored as JSONL files in `.aether-tasks/`. See [Tools](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/) for configuration.

## Tools

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `task_create` | Create a new task or subtask |
| `task_get` | Get a task by ID |
| `task_list` | List tasks with filters |
| `task_update` | Update task status, title, or other fields |

### task_create

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `title` | `string` | Task title (required) |
| `description` | `string` | Detailed description |
| `parent` | `string` | Parent task ID (creates a subtask) |
| `assignee` | `string` | Agent or user name |
| `dependencies` | `string[]` | Task IDs that must complete first |

### task_list

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `assignee` | `string` | Filter by assignee |
| `status` | `string` | Filter by status |
| `tree_id` | `string` | Filter by root task tree |
| `ready_only` | `boolean` | Only tasks with all dependencies met |

### task_update

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `id` | `string` | Task ID (required) |
| `title` | `string` | New title |
| `description` | `string` | New description |
| `status` | `string` | New status |
| `assignee` | `string` | New assignee |
| `dependencies` | `string[]` | Updated dependencies |

## Task IDs

- Root tasks: `at-a1b2c3d4` (prefix + 8-char hash)
- Subtasks: `at-a1b2c3d4.1`, `at-a1b2c3d4.2`, etc.

## Status values

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `Pending` | Not yet started |
| `InProgress` | Currently being worked on |
| `Completed` | Done |
| `Blocked` | Waiting on dependencies |

## Research metadata

Tasks support structured metadata for tracking research progress:

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `summary` | High-level summary of findings |
| `decisions` | Key decisions made |
| `facts` | Important facts discovered |
| `next_steps` | What to do next |
| `blockers` | What's blocking progress |
| `files_read` | Files that were examined |
| `resources` | External resources referenced |

import { FileTree } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Agents are defined in `.aether/settings.json` — their model, prompts, tools, and how they can be invoked.

## File location

<FileTree>

- .aether/
  - **settings.json** — Agent catalog
  - mcp.json — MCP server config
  - SYSTEM.md — Base system prompt
- AGENTS.md — Project instructions

</FileTree>

## Example

```json
{
  "prompts": ["AGENTS.md"],
  "mcpServers": ".aether/mcp.json",
  "agents": [
    {
      "name": "coder",
      "description": "General-purpose coding assistant",
      "model": "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5",
      "userInvocable": true
    }
  ]
}
```

## Top-level fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `prompts` | `string[]` | Glob patterns for prompt files inherited by all agents |
| `mcpServers` | `string` | Path to inherited MCP config (relative to project root) |
| `agents` | `AgentEntry[]` | Array of agent definitions |

## Agent entry fields

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
| `name` | `string` | *required* | Unique identifier for the agent |
| `description` | `string` | *required* | Human-readable description shown in the UI |
| `model` | `string` | *required* | Model spec — `provider:model-id` or comma-separated [alloy](/aether/configuration/llm-providers/#alloying) |
| `reasoningEffort` | `string` | — | `"low"`, `"medium"`, `"high"`, or `"xhigh"` |
| `userInvocable` | `boolean` | `false` | Appears as a mode in the TUI and ACP clients |
| `agentInvocable` | `boolean` | `false` | Can be spawned as a [sub-agent](/aether/built-in-servers/subagents/) |
| `prompts` | `string[]` | `[]` | Glob patterns for agent-specific prompt files |
| `mcpServers` | `string` | — | Override MCP config path for this agent |
| `tools` | `object` | `{}` | [Tool filtering](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/#tool-filtering) with `allow` and `deny` arrays |

## Validation rules

:::note
- Agent names must be unique (whitespace is trimmed)
- `"__default__"` is a reserved name
- At least one of `userInvocable` or `agentInvocable` must be `true`
- All prompt glob patterns must match at least one file
- MCP config paths must point to existing files
:::
Configure models from Anthropic, OpenRouter, Ollama, and more.

Aether supports many LLM providers out of the box. And, you can provide your own via implementing a `trait`.

You can specify the model an agent uses in your [agent settings](/aether/configuration/agent-settings/) file.

```diff title="settings.json"
 {
   "name": "my-agent",
+  "model": "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5"
 }
```

In the TUI, only LLM providers with configured credentials appear in the model selector.

## Providers

### Anthropic

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `anthropic:<model-id>` |

### OpenRouter

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `openrouter:<vendor>/<model-id>` |

OpenRouter proxies 100+ models from various vendors. Use the `vendor/model` format from their [model list](https://openrouter.ai/models).

### OpenAI

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `OPENAI_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `openai:<model-id>` |

### Codex

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | OAuth login — no API key needed |
| **Model syntax** | `codex:<model-id>` |

Codex authenticates through a browser-based OAuth flow with OpenAI. On first use, Aether opens a browser window to complete the login. Credentials are stored securely in your OS keychain — you only need to log in once.

In the model selector, Codex models show a **"Needs login"** badge until you've authenticated.

### Gemini

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `GEMINI_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `gemini:<model-id>` |

### DeepSeek

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `deepseek:<model-id>` |

### AWS Bedrock

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | AWS credential chain (environment, config file, or IAM role) |
| **Model syntax** | `bedrock:<model-id>` |

### Ollama

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | None — requires a running [Ollama](https://ollama.com) server |
| **Model syntax** | `ollama:<model-id>` |

Aether auto-discovers models from your Ollama instance. Any model you've pulled with `ollama pull` will appear in the model selector.

Set `OLLAMA_HOST` to override the default address (`http://localhost:11434`).

### Llama.cpp

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | None — requires a running [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp) server |
| **Model syntax** | `llamacpp:<model-id>` |

Llama.cpp serves a single model at a time. Aether queries the server's `/v1/models` endpoint to discover the loaded model, which then appears in the model selector.

Set `LLAMA_CPP_HOST` to override the default address (`http://localhost:8080`).

### Moonshot (Kimi)

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `moonshot:<model-id>` |

### ZAI (Zhipu)

| | |
|---|---|
| **Credentials** | `ZAI_API_KEY` |
| **Model syntax** | `zai:<model-id>` |

### Bring your own

Aether's `llm` crate exposes the `StreamingModelProvider` trait, so you can integrate any LLM backend:

```rust
pub trait StreamingModelProvider: Send + Sync {
    fn stream_response(&self, context: &Context) -> LlmResponseStream;
    fn display_name(&self) -> String;
    fn context_window(&self) -> Option<u32>;
    fn model(&self) -> Option<LlmModel> { None }
}
```

Implement this trait on your own struct, and it can be passed directly to the agent builder. See the [Custom Providers](/libraries/llm/custom-providers/) guide for a full walkthrough with examples.

## Reasoning effort

Some models support extended thinking. Set `reasoningEffort` on an agent to control the thinking budget:

| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `"low"` | Minimal thinking — fastest responses |
| `"medium"` | Moderate thinking |
| `"high"` | Extended thinking for complex tasks |
| `"xhigh"` | Maximum thinking budget |

```json
{
  "name": "deep-thinker",
  "model": "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5",
  "reasoningEffort": "high",
  "..."
}
```

## Alloying

Model alloying lets you combine multiple LLM providers into a single agent by comma-separating model specs. Each turn uses the next model in the list, cycling through in round-robin order.

### Syntax

```json
"model": "provider1:model1,provider2:model2,provider3:model3"
```

### Example

An agent that alternates between DeepSeek and Anthropic:

```json
{
  "name": "alloy-coder",
  "description": "Cost-optimized coding agent",
  "model": "deepseek:deepseek-chat,anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5",
  "userInvocable": true
}
```

Turn 1 uses DeepSeek, turn 2 uses Anthropic, turn 3 uses DeepSeek, and so on.

### Use cases

- **Cost optimization** — Alternate between expensive and cheaper models. Use a powerful model for complex turns and a lighter one for simple follow-ups.
- **Redundancy** — If one provider has an outage, the other turns still work.
- **Comparison** — See how different models handle the same conversation context.

### Considerations

- Each model in the alloy must have its corresponding API key set
- Reasoning effort applies to all models in the alloy (models that don't support it will ignore it)
- The conversation context is shared across all models — each model sees the full history regardless of which model generated previous turns
Configure the MCP servers that give agents their tools.
Control agent behavior with prompt files.

import { FileTree } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Aether assembles system prompts from multiple sources — markdown files on disk, glob patterns in your config, and MCP server instructions — into a single system prompt for each agent.

## Prompt sources

Prompts are assembled in this order:

1. **Inherited prompts** — `prompts` array at the top level of `settings.json`, applied to all agents
2. **Agent-specific prompts** — `prompts` array on individual agent entries
3. **MCP instructions** — Prompts provided by MCP servers during initialization

## Prompt files

The `prompts` field in `settings.json` accepts glob patterns that resolve to markdown files relative to the project root. Top-level patterns are inherited by all agents; agent-specific patterns add to (not replace) the inherited set.

```json title=".aether/settings.json"
{
  "prompts": ["AGENTS.md", "prompts/*.md"],
  "agents": [
    {
      "name": "coder",
      "prompts": [".aether/agents/coder/AGENTS.md"],
      "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

A common convention is to place an `AGENTS.md` at the project root with shared instructions:

```markdown title="AGENTS.md"
# Project Agent

You are a coding assistant for this Rust project.

## Rules
- Always read files before editing them
- Write tests for new functionality
- Use `cargo clippy` to check for warnings
```

:::note
Every glob pattern must match at least one file. Aether reports a validation error if a pattern matches nothing.
:::

## Debugging prompts

Use `show-prompt` to see the fully assembled system prompt for any agent:

```bash
aether show-prompt
aether show-prompt --agent researcher
aether show-prompt -C /path/to/project
```
Use Aether as an AI agent inside your editor.

import { Steps } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Aether can run as an ACP (Agent Client Protocol) server, integrating directly into editors that support the protocol. Currently, [Zed](https://zed.dev) is the primary supported editor.

## Zed setup

<Steps>

1. **Install Aether**

   ```bash
   cargo install aether-agent-cli
   ```

   Or build from source:

   ```bash
   cargo build --release -p aether-agent-cli
   ```

2. **Configure Zed**

   Add the following to your Zed `settings.json`:

   ```json
   {
     "agent_servers": {
       "Aether Agent": {
         "command": "/path/to/aether",
         "args": ["acp"],
         "env": {
           "RUST_LOG": "info",
           "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY": "your-key-here"
         }
       }
     }
   }
   ```

3. **Open an agent thread**

   In Zed, open the Agent Panel and select "New Aether Agent Thread" to start a conversation.

</Steps>

## ACP server flags

| Flag | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `--log-dir` | `path` | `/tmp/aether-acp-logs` | Log output directory |

## Logging

ACP server logs are written to the `--log-dir` directory. Increase verbosity with `RUST_LOG`:

```json
{
  "env": {
    "RUST_LOG": "debug"
  }
}
```

:::tip
Check the logs at `/tmp/aether-acp-logs/` if the agent isn't responding or tools aren't working as expected.
:::
Run single prompts from the command line.

import { Tabs, TabItem } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The headless CLI runs a single prompt and exits — ideal for scripting, CI pipelines, and automation.

```bash
aether headless "Explain the main function in this project"
```

## Flags

| Flag | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `[PROMPT]...` | positional | — | Prompt text (joined with spaces) |
| `-m, --model` | `string` | — | Model spec (e.g., `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5`) |
| `-a, --agent` | `string` | — | Named agent from settings.json |
| `-C, --cwd` | `path` | `.` | Working directory |
| `--mcp-config` | `path` | — | Path to MCP config file |
| `--system-prompt` | `string` | — | Additional system prompt text |
| `--output` | `string` | `text` | Output format: `text`, `pretty`, `json` |
| `-v, --verbose` | flag | — | Verbose logging to stderr |

:::note
`--model` and `--agent` are mutually exclusive. If neither is set, Aether uses the first `userInvocable` agent from settings.json.
:::

## Output formats

<Tabs>
  <TabItem label="text">
    Plain text output — just the agent's response.

    ```bash
    aether headless --output text "What does this project do?"
    ```
  </TabItem>
  <TabItem label="pretty">
    Formatted output with markdown rendering.

    ```bash
    aether headless --output pretty "Summarize the README"
    ```
  </TabItem>
  <TabItem label="json">
    Structured JSON output — useful for piping into other tools.

    ```bash
    aether headless --output json "List all TODO comments" | jq '.response'
    ```
  </TabItem>
</Tabs>

## Reading from stdin

If no prompt is given and stdin is not a TTY, Aether reads the prompt from stdin:

```bash
echo "Explain this code" | aether headless
```

```bash
cat error.log | aether headless "What went wrong?"
```

This is useful for piping file contents or command output into Aether.

## Examples

### Quick question with a specific model

```bash
aether headless -m anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-5 "What does the Config struct do?"
```

### Using a named agent

```bash
aether headless -a researcher "Find all API endpoints in this project"
```

### CI pipeline

```bash
aether headless \
  -m anthropic:claude-haiku-4-5 \
  --output json \
  "Review the staged changes and list any issues" \
  > review.json
```

### Different working directory

```bash
aether headless -C /path/to/project "Summarize the architecture"
```

## Other subcommands

### show-prompt

Print the fully assembled system prompt (useful for debugging):

```bash
aether show-prompt
aether show-prompt --agent researcher
```

### agent new

Initialize a new Aether project:

```bash
aether agent new [--path <dir>]
```

See [Getting Started](/getting-started/overview/) for details.
Review changes and add inline comments with the two-pane diff viewer.

Press `Ctrl+G` to open the git diff view. It shows a two-pane layout: changed files on the left, unified diff on the right. You can navigate the diff and add inline comments that get sent to the agent as a structured prompt.

## Layout

```
┌─────────────────┬───────────────────────────┐
│  Changed Files   │  Unified Diff             │
│                  │                           │
│  ● src/main.rs   │  @@ -10,6 +10,8 @@       │
│    src/lib.rs    │   fn main() {             │
│    Cargo.toml    │  +    let config = ...     │
│                  │  +    setup(config);       │
│                  │       run();               │
│                  │   }                        │
│                  │                           │
├─────────────────┴───────────────────────────┤
│  > Comment input (when active)              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Navigation

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `h` / `←` | Focus file list (left pane) |
| `l` / `→` or `Enter` | Focus diff (right pane) |
| `j` / `↓` | Move down |
| `k` / `↑` | Move up |
| `g g` | Jump to top |
| `G` | Jump to bottom |
| `c` | Add comment on current line |
| `Enter` | Submit all queued comments |
| `r` | Refresh the diff |
| `Esc` | Close git diff view |

## Inline comments

1. Navigate to a diff line you want to comment on
2. Press `c` to open the comment input
3. Type your comment and press `Enter` to queue it
4. Navigate to other lines and add more comments
5. Press `Enter` (when not in comment input) to submit all comments

All queued comments are sent as a single prompt to the agent, including the file path, line number, and surrounding diff context. This lets the agent understand exactly which changes you're commenting on.

## Use case

The git diff view is designed for reviewing agent-generated changes. After the agent modifies files, open the diff to:

- See exactly what changed
- Add targeted feedback on specific lines
- Request fixes to particular hunks without describing them in prose
Keyboard shortcuts, slash commands, and file attachments.

## Keybindings

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `Enter` | Send message |
| `Esc` | Cancel current operation / close modal |
| `Ctrl+C` | Exit |
| `Tab` | Cycle reasoning effort (off → low → medium → high) |
| `Shift+Tab` | Cycle agent mode |
| `/` | Open command picker |
| `@` | Open file picker |
| `Ctrl+G` | Toggle [git diff view](/aether/terminal/git-diff/) |

## Slash commands

Type `/` to open the command picker. It supports fuzzy search — just start typing to filter.

### Built-in commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `/clear` | Start a new [session](/aether/terminal/sessions/) |
| `/settings` | Open the [settings overlay](/aether/terminal/settings/) |
| `/resume` | Resume a previous [session](/aether/terminal/sessions/) |

### Agent-provided commands

Agents can register additional slash commands. These appear in the picker alongside built-in commands. Each command has a name, description, and optional argument hint.

## File picker

Type `@` to open the file picker. It indexes files in the working directory (respecting `.gitignore`) and supports fuzzy search.

- Up to 50,000 files indexed
- Respects `.gitignore`, `.git/info/exclude`, and global gitignore
- Select a file to attach it to your next message

### How attachments work

Selected files are read and sent to the agent as content blocks alongside your message:

- Maximum file size: 1 MB
- Files are converted to UTF-8
- Non-UTF-8 files are flagged with a warning
- Truncated files include a notice

You can attach multiple files to a single message by using `@` multiple times before sending.
Run Aether interactively in the terminal.

The default way to use Aether is through its built-in terminal UI — a full-screen interactive interface for chatting with agents, reviewing diffs, and managing sessions.

```bash
aether
```

## Features

- **Streaming responses** — See model output as it arrives with syntax highlighting
- **Tool tracking** — Live progress for running tool calls and sub-agents
- **Plan visualization** — Task plans with status updates
- **Inline diffs** — Review file changes in a two-pane [diff viewer](/aether/terminal/git-diff/)
- **Slash commands** — Trigger skills and commands with `/name`
- **File attachments** — Attach files to prompts via `@` picker
- **Settings overlay** — Configure models, reasoning, MCP servers
- **Session persistence** — Resume previous conversations

## UI layout

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Conversation                            │
│  ├─ User messages                        │
│  ├─ Agent responses (streaming markdown) │
│  ├─ Tool calls (with progress)           │
│  └─ Plan entries                         │
│                                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Prompt input          [@file] [/command]│
├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  agent · mode · model   ░░░░ · 45%      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The status line at the bottom shows the agent name, current mode, model, reasoning effort level, and context usage percentage.

## Logging

```bash
# Default location
/tmp/wisp-logs/wisp.log.2026-03-25

# Custom directory
aether --log-dir ./logs

# Verbose logging
RUST_LOG=debug aether
```

Sessions represent a conversation with an agent. Each session has its own message history, configuration state, and working directory.

## How sessions work

Sessions are managed by the agent, not the terminal UI. When you start Aether, it asks the agent to create a new session. The agent stores the session state — messages, config, and context — and the UI renders it.

## Starting a new session

```
/clear
```

This tells the agent to create a fresh session. The conversation history is cleared and configuration options are reset.

## Resuming a session

```
/resume
```

This asks the agent for a list of previous sessions. A picker appears showing available sessions with their titles. Select one to restore it — the conversation history and configuration are loaded from the agent.

## Session state

Each session tracks:

| State | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| Session ID | Unique identifier |
| Title | Agent-generated summary |
| Working directory | The directory the session was started in |
| Config selections | Model, reasoning effort, mode, etc. |
| Conversation history | Full message history (stored by agent) |

When you switch sessions, the configuration options that were active in that session are restored.

## Multiple sessions

You can have many sessions stored by the agent. Use `/resume` to switch between them. Only one session is active at a time.
Configure appearance and agent options in the terminal UI.

import { FileTree } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The terminal UI has two layers of settings: its own UI preferences (themes), and agent configuration options (models, reasoning, servers) that the agent advertises through ACP.

## UI settings

Stored in `~/.wisp/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "theme": {
    "file": "catppuccin.tmTheme"
  }
}
```

Override the home directory with `WISP_HOME`:

```bash
export WISP_HOME=~/my-wisp-config
```

## Themes

Themes are TextMate `.tmTheme` files. Place them in `~/.wisp/themes/` and reference by filename in settings.

<FileTree>
- ~/.wisp/
  - settings.json
  - themes/
    - catppuccin.tmTheme
    - dracula.tmTheme
    - nord.tmTheme
</FileTree>

The theme colors the entire UI: conversation text, syntax highlighting, diff rendering, status line, and overlays.

## Settings overlay

Open with `/settings`. The overlay shows all configurable options:

- **Model** — Select which LLM model to use (if the agent supports multiple)
- **Reasoning effort** — Set thinking budget (also cyclable with `Tab`)
- **Mode** — Switch agent modes (also cyclable with `Shift+Tab`)
- **MCP servers** — View connected servers and their status
- **Provider logins** — Authenticate with providers that require it

These options are advertised by the agent — the terminal UI discovers them at session start. Different agents may expose different options.

## How agent config works

The terminal UI doesn't know about models or reasoning levels directly. Instead:

1. The agent sends `SessionConfigOption` entries describing available settings
2. The UI renders them in the settings overlay
3. When you change a value, the selection is sent back to the agent
4. The agent applies the change

The `Tab` (reasoning) and `Shift+Tab` (mode) shortcuts cycle through the most common agent-provided options.

## Authentication

Some agents or MCP servers require authentication. The settings overlay shows:

- **Provider logins** — Start OAuth flows or enter API keys
- **MCP server auth** — Authenticate individual MCP servers

Authentication state is managed by the agent, not stored locally.
What Aether is and why it exists.

Aether is a modular, open-source AI coding agent toolkit written in Rust.


### Design principles

- **Empty by default** — Agents have no default system prompt or tools. You add what you need.
- **Any model** — Use any LLM you want, local or remote. Anthropic, OpenAI, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z, Moonshot, LlamaCpp, Ollama, and more are supported out of the box.
- **Any tools** — Agents get tools via [MCP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) servers. You can author tools in any language.
- **Any surface** — Run your agent in a TUI, IDE/editor (via ACP) or headless.
Install Aether and run your first prompt.

import { Tabs, TabItem, Steps, FileTree, CardGrid, LinkCard } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

<Steps>

1. **Install**

   <Tabs>
     <TabItem label="cargo install">
       ```bash
       cargo install aether-agent-cli
       ```
     </TabItem>
     <TabItem label="Build from source">
       ```bash
       git clone https://github.com/joshka/aether.git
       cd aether
       cargo build --release
       ```

       Binaries are written to `target/release/aether` and `target/release/wisp`.
     </TabItem>
   </Tabs>

   Requires **Rust 1.85+** — install via [rustup](https://rustup.rs) if you don't have it.

2. **Create your first agent**

   ```bash
   cd your-project
   aether agent new
   ```

   ```
   ✓ Created .aether/settings.json
   ✓ Created .aether/mcp.json
   ✓ Created .aether/SYSTEM.md
   ✓ Created AGENTS.md
   ```

   <FileTree>
     - .aether/
       - settings.json — [Agent definitions](/aether/configuration/agent-settings/)
       - mcp.json — [MCP server config](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/)
       - SYSTEM.md — Base system prompt
     - AGENTS.md — [Project instructions](/aether/configuration/system-prompts/)
   </FileTree>

   :::note[Set up your LLM credentials]
   Most providers need an API key (e.g. `export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...`).
   See [LLM Providers](/aether/configuration/llm-providers/) for all supported providers and credential setup.
   :::

3. **Run it**

   **Interactive TUI**

   ```bash
   aether
   ```

   The default experience — a terminal interface for conversations with your agent.

   **IDE / editor (via ACP)**

   ```bash
   aether acp
   ```

   Exposes agents over [ACP](https://agentclientprotocol.org/) for [editor integration](/aether/running/editor-integration/).

   **Headless**

   ```bash
   aether headless "Explain the main function in this project"
   ```

   Run a single prompt and exit — ideal for scripts and CI. See [Headless CLI](/aether/running/headless/) for all flags and output formats.

</Steps>

Create and run agents programmatically with aether-core.
Handle agent events and streaming responses.
Connect MCP servers to agents programmatically.

The `McpBuilder` connects MCP servers — both built-in and external — to your agent. It handles server lifecycle, tool discovery, and message routing.

```rust
use aether_core::mcp::mcp;
```

## McpBuilder

```rust
let mcp_result = mcp()
    .register_in_memory_server("coding", server_factory)
    .from_json_file(".aether/mcp.json").await?
    .with_roots(vec![PathBuf::from(".")])
    .spawn()
    .await?;
```

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `register_in_memory_server(name, factory)` | Add a built-in MCP server |
| `from_json_file(path)` | Load servers from a JSON config file |
| `with_servers(configs)` | Add servers from a `Vec<McpServerConfig>` |
| `with_roots(paths)` | Set workspace roots for server discovery |
| `with_oauth_handler(handler)` | Set OAuth handler for authenticated servers |
| `spawn()` | Start all servers and discover tools |

## McpSpawnResult

`spawn()` returns everything needed to wire tools into an agent:

```rust
pub struct McpSpawnResult {
    pub tool_definitions: Vec<ToolDefinition>,
    pub instructions: Vec<ServerInstructions>,
    pub server_statuses: Vec<McpServerStatusEntry>,
    pub command_tx: Sender<McpCommand>,
    pub elicitation_rx: Receiver<ElicitationRequest>,
    pub handle: JoinHandle<()>,
}
```

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `tool_definitions` | Tools discovered from all servers — pass to `AgentBuilder::tools()` |
| `instructions` | Server-provided system prompt additions |
| `command_tx` | Channel for routing tool calls — pass to `AgentBuilder::tools()` |
| `handle` | Background task handle for the MCP manager |

## Wiring tools to an agent

```rust
use aether_core::core::agent;
use aether_core::mcp::mcp;

// 1. Spawn MCP servers
let mcp_result = mcp()
    .from_json_file(".aether/mcp.json").await?
    .spawn()
    .await?;

// 2. Create agent with tools
let (user_tx, agent_rx, handle) = agent(provider)
    .system_prompt(Prompt::text("You are a coding assistant."))
    .tools(mcp_result.command_tx, mcp_result.tool_definitions)
    .spawn()
    .await?;
```

## Using in-memory servers

Built-in servers from the `mcp_servers` crate can be registered directly:

```rust
use mcp_servers::CodingMcp;

let mcp_result = mcp()
    .register_in_memory_server("coding", || {
        CodingMcp::new()
            .with_root_dir("/my/project".into())
            .into_dyn()
    })
    .spawn()
    .await?;
```

See [Embedding Servers](/libraries/mcp-servers/embedding/) for details on constructing built-in servers.

## Loading from config

`from_json_file()` reads the standard MCP config format (`.aether/mcp.json`):

```rust
let mcp_result = mcp()
    .from_json_file(".aether/mcp.json").await?
    .spawn()
    .await?;
```

This handles both in-memory and stdio servers defined in the config. See [MCP Servers](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/) for the config format.

Aether is organized as a Cargo workspace with focused, single-responsibility packages. Each package can be used independently or composed together.

## Package map

| Package | Crate | Purpose |
|---------|-------|---------|
| **aether-core** | `aether_core` | Agent runtime — conversation loop, context management, tool dispatch, streaming |
| **llm** | `llm` | Unified LLM provider interface — Anthropic, OpenRouter, OpenAI, DeepSeek, Gemini, Ollama, Bedrock, and more |
| **mcp-servers** | `mcp_servers` | Built-in MCP tool servers — coding (files, bash, search, LSP), skills, tasks, sub-agents, survey |
| **aether-cli** | `aether_cli` | CLI binaries — headless mode, ACP server, init wizard, show-prompt |
| **aether-project** | `aether_project` | Project configuration — loads and validates `settings.json`, resolves MCP configs and prompt globs |
| **wisp** | `wisp` | Interactive TUI — markdown rendering, syntax highlighting, inline diffs, command picker |
| **crucible** | `crucible` | Evaluation framework — assertion-based testing of agent behavior |
| **mcp-utils** | `mcp_utils` | MCP client utilities — connecting to and managing external MCP servers |
| **tui** | `tui` | Low-level TUI rendering primitives used by Wisp |
| **acp-utils** | `acp_utils` | Agent Client Protocol utilities — client and server helpers |
| **aether-lspd** | `aether_lspd` | LSP server daemon — standalone language server integration |
| **llm-codegen** | `llm_codegen` | Code generation for LLM provider model catalogs |
| **utils** | `utils` | Shared utilities used across packages |

## Dependency flow

```
aether-cli
├── aether-core
│   ├── llm (provider interface)
│   ├── mcp-servers (built-in tools)
│   └── mcp-utils (external server connections)
├── aether-project (config loading)
└── wisp
    └── tui (rendering primitives)

crucible (standalone — uses aether-core for eval runs)
```

## Key design decisions

- **MCP for all tools** — There is no built-in tool system. Every tool comes through MCP, whether from in-memory servers or external processes. This keeps the core runtime minimal and tools composable.
- **Provider abstraction** — The `llm` crate defines a unified interface. Adding a new provider means implementing a single trait, not touching the agent runtime.
- **Config as data** — Agent definitions, tool access, and prompt composition are all declarative JSON/markdown. No code changes needed to reconfigure agents.
- **Workspace isolation** — Each package has a focused responsibility. You can use `llm` without `mcp-servers`, or `aether-core` without `wisp`.

## Library documentation

Each package with a public API has its own documentation section:

- [**aether-core**](/libraries/aether-core/agent-builder/) — Agent builder, events, MCP integration
- [**llm**](/libraries/llm/provider-interface/) — Provider trait, creating and implementing providers
- [**mcp-servers**](/libraries/mcp-servers/embedding/) — Embedding built-in servers in custom apps
- [**tui**](/libraries/tui/components/) — Component trait, widgets, rendering
- [**crucible**](/libraries/crucible/evals/) — Evaluation framework for testing agent behavior

## Related docs

- [Agent Settings](/aether/configuration/agent-settings/) — How to configure agents (CLI)
- [MCP Servers](/aether/configuration/mcp-servers/) — How to configure tools (CLI)
- [LLM Providers](/aether/configuration/llm-providers/) — Supported models and providers
Test agent behavior with the Crucible evaluation framework.
The StreamingModelProvider trait and core LLM types.

The `llm` crate provides a unified interface for streaming LLM responses across providers. The core abstraction is the `StreamingModelProvider` trait.

```toml title="Cargo.toml"
[dependencies]
llm = "0.1"
```

## StreamingModelProvider

```rust
pub trait StreamingModelProvider: Send + Sync {
    fn stream_response(&self, context: &Context) -> LlmResponseStream;
    fn display_name(&self) -> String;
    fn context_window(&self) -> Option<u32>;
    fn model(&self) -> Option<LlmModel> { None }
}
```

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `stream_response(context)` | Stream a response for the given conversation context |
| `display_name()` | Human-readable provider + model name |
| `context_window()` | Max context size in tokens (if known) |
| `model()` | The model catalog entry (optional) |

`LlmResponseStream` is `Pin<Box<dyn Stream<Item = Result<LlmResponse>> + Send>>`.

## Creating a provider

Use `ProviderFactory` to create providers from environment variables:

```rust
use llm::{ProviderFactory, providers::AnthropicProvider};

let provider = AnthropicProvider::from_env()?
    .with_model("claude-sonnet-4-5");
```

Available providers: `AnthropicProvider`, `OpenAiProvider`, `OpenRouterProvider`, `GeminiProvider`, `OllamaProvider`, `LlamaCppProvider`, `BedrockProvider` (feature: `bedrock`), `CodexProvider` (feature: `codex`).

## Core types

### Context

The conversation state passed to `stream_response`:

```rust
let mut ctx = Context::new(messages, tools);
ctx.set_reasoning_effort(Some(ReasoningEffort::High));
ctx.set_prompt_cache_key(Some("my-cache".into()));
```

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `new(messages, tools)` | Create from messages and tool definitions |
| `add_message(msg)` | Append a message |
| `set_tools(tools)` | Replace tool definitions |
| `set_reasoning_effort(effort)` | Set thinking budget |
| `messages()` | Get all messages |
| `tools()` | Get tool definitions |
| `estimated_token_count()` | Rough token estimate |

### ChatMessage

```rust
pub enum ChatMessage {
    System { content, timestamp },
    User { content, timestamp },
    Assistant { content, reasoning, timestamp, tool_calls },
    ToolCallResult(Result<ToolCallResult, ToolCallError>),
    Error { message, timestamp },
    Summary { content, timestamp, messages_compacted },
}
```

### LlmResponse

Chunks emitted by `stream_response`:

| Variant | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `Start { message_id }` | New response started |
| `Text { chunk }` | Text content chunk |
| `Reasoning { chunk }` | Extended thinking chunk |
| `ToolRequestStart { id, name }` | Tool call beginning |
| `ToolRequestArg { id, chunk }` | Streaming tool arguments |
| `ToolRequestComplete { tool_call }` | Tool call fully formed |
| `Usage { input_tokens, output_tokens, cached_input_tokens }` | Token usage |
| `Done { stop_reason }` | Response complete |
| `Error { message }` | Error during streaming |

### StopReason

Why the model stopped generating:

`EndTurn`, `Length`, `ToolCalls`, `ContentFilter`, `Error`, `Unknown(String)`

### ToolDefinition

```rust
pub struct ToolDefinition {
    pub name: String,
    pub description: String,
    pub parameters: String,  // JSON Schema
    pub server: Option<String>,
}
```

## Error handling

```rust
pub enum LlmError {
    MissingApiKey(String),
    InvalidApiKey(String),
    ApiRequest(String),
    ApiError(String),
    ContextOverflow(ContextOverflowError),
    IoError(String),
    JsonParsing(String),
    ToolParameterParsing { tool_name, error },
    OAuthError(String),
    Other(String),
}
```

`ContextOverflowError` provides `requested_tokens` and `max_tokens` for handling context limits.
Use built-in MCP servers as library components.
The Component trait and built-in TUI widgets.
Frame-based terminal rendering with diff updates.
Use Wisp as a library in your own application.

Wisp can be embedded in other Rust applications. There are two entry points depending on how much control you need.

```toml title="Cargo.toml"
[dependencies]
wisp = "0.1"
```

## `run_tui` — one-shot launch

The simplest way to embed Wisp. Pass an agent command string and Wisp handles everything: subprocess spawning, ACP handshake, session creation, and the TUI event loop.

```rust
use wisp::run_tui;

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> Result<(), wisp::AppError> {
    wisp::setup_logging(None);
    run_tui("my-agent serve").await
}
```

The command string is split and executed as a subprocess. The agent must speak ACP over stdin/stdout.

## `run_with_state` — custom initialization

For more control, initialize `RuntimeState` yourself and pass it in:

```rust
use wisp::{run_with_state, RuntimeState};

let state = RuntimeState {
    session_id,
    agent_name: "my-agent".into(),
    config_options: vec![],
    auth_methods: vec![],
    theme,
    event_rx,
    prompt_handle,
    working_dir: std::env::current_dir()?,
};

run_with_state(state).await?;
```

This is useful when you want to:
- Pre-configure the ACP connection
- Use a custom transport (not stdin/stdout subprocess)
- Set up logging or error handling differently
- Run initialization logic before the TUI starts

## RuntimeState

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `session_id` | `SessionId` | Active session identifier |
| `agent_name` | `String` | Agent's display name (shown in status line) |
| `config_options` | `Vec<SessionConfigOption>` | Agent-advertised settings |
| `auth_methods` | `Vec<AuthMethod>` | Available authentication methods |
| `theme` | `Theme` | UI color theme |
| `event_rx` | `UnboundedReceiver<AcpEvent>` | Channel receiving events from agent |
| `prompt_handle` | `AcpPromptHandle` | Handle for sending prompts and commands |
| `working_dir` | `PathBuf` | Working directory for file operations |
Run the Wisp terminal UI as a standalone client for any ACP-compatible agent.

Wisp is the terminal UI that powers the `aether` command. It can also be installed and run standalone to work with any agent that speaks the [Agent Client Protocol (ACP)](https://github.com/anthropics/agent-client-protocol).

## Install

```bash
cargo install aether-wisp
```

## Usage

```bash
# Default: uses Aether as the backend agent
wisp

# Use a custom ACP agent
wisp --agent "my-custom-agent serve"
```

The `--agent` flag specifies the command Wisp runs as a subprocess. The agent must speak ACP over stdin/stdout.

## CLI flags

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--agent <command>` | `aether acp` | Agent subprocess command |
| `--log-dir <path>` | `/tmp/wisp-logs` | Log output directory |

## Building an ACP-compatible agent

Any process that speaks ACP over stdin/stdout can be used with Wisp. The handshake:

1. Wisp sends `InitializeRequest` with client info
2. Agent responds with its name and capabilities
3. Wisp sends `NewSessionRequest` with the working directory
4. Agent responds with session ID and config options
5. Normal conversation begins — prompts in, events out

See the [Agent Client Protocol](https://github.com/anthropics/agent-client-protocol) specification for the full protocol details.
